summary: Typosquat of postcss-selector-parser shipping AES-GCM-encrypted eval payload
details: Package name impersonates `postcss-selector-parser` (a widely-used PostCSS dependency). `src/selector-parser.js` re-exports the real `postcss-selector-parser` and `src/index.js` re-exports the same surface as `selectorParser`/`postcssSelectorParser` to provide a convincing cover API. Alongside that cover, the package ships a ~13KB AES-GCM ciphertext (`DEFAULT_FINAL_ENCODED_TEXT` in `src/config/defaults.js`) together with the decryption passphrase (`DEFAULT_AES_PASSPHRASE = "default-dev-passphrase"`) and salt (`"encode-npm-c-salt"`) in the same module. The exported `run()` function (`runDefaultDecodedFunction` in `src/pipeline/custom-codec-pipeline.js`) decrypts the blob and executes the plaintext via `new Function("require", runnable)(require)`. The encrypted blob plus co-located key serves no functional purpose other than hiding the payload from scanners and reviewers. The package's `npm start` / `npm run decode` scripts invoke `runtime/lib.min.js`, which calls `require('../src').run()`, triggering the eval path on those commands; any consumer that requires the package and calls `run()` is also exposed. The combination of typosquat cover, encrypted-with-shipped-key payload, and `new Function()` execution is an obfuscated remote-code-execution backdoor.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
